**Astrocytes** (from [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") [ἄστρον](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%84%CF%83%CF%84%CF%81%CE%BF%CE%BD "wikt:ἄστρον"), *ástron*, "star" and [κύτος](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BA%CF%8D%CF%84%CE%BF%CF%82 "wikt:κύτος"), *kútos*, "cavity", "cell"), also known collectively as **astroglia**, are characteristic star-shaped [glial cells](/wiki/Glial_cells "Glial cells") in the [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") and [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord"). They perform many functions, including biochemical control of [endothelial cells](/wiki/Endothelial_cell "Endothelial cell") that form the [blood–brain barrier](/wiki/Blood%E2%80%93brain_barrier "Blood–brain barrier"),[[1]](#cite_note-Suzuki_Sa_Ochiai_Mullins_2014_pp._755–796-1) provision of nutrients to the nervous tissue, maintenance of extracellular ion balance, regulation of cerebral blood flow, and a role in the repair and [scarring](/wiki/Glial_scar "Glial scar") process of the brain and spinal cord following infection and traumatic injuries.[[2]](#cite_note-Freeman-2) The proportion of astrocytes in the brain is not well defined; depending on the counting technique used, studies have found that the astrocyte proportion varies by region and ranges from 20% to around 40% of all glia.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Another study reports that astrocytes are the most numerous cell type in the brain.[[2]](#cite_note-Freeman-2) Astrocytes are the major source of [cholesterol](/wiki/Cholesterol "Cholesterol") in the central nervous system.[[4]](#cite_note-biorxiv.org-4) [Apolipoprotein E](/wiki/Apolipoprotein_E "Apolipoprotein E") transports cholesterol from astrocytes to neurons and other glial cells, regulating [cell signaling](/wiki/Cell_signaling "Cell signaling") in the brain.[[4]](#cite_note-biorxiv.org-4) Astrocytes in humans are more than twenty times larger than in rodent brains, and make contact with more than ten times the number of synapses.[[5]](#cite_note-Sloan-5)

Research since the mid-1990s has shown that astrocytes propagate intercellular [Ca2+](/wiki/Calcium "Calcium") waves over long distances in response to stimulation, and, similar to neurons, release transmitters (called [gliotransmitters](/wiki/Gliotransmitter "Gliotransmitter")) in a Ca2+-dependent manner.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Data suggest that astrocytes also signal to neurons through Ca2+-dependent release of [glutamate](/wiki/Glutamate "Glutamate").[[7]](#cite_note-pmid18834310-7) Such discoveries have made astrocytes an important area of research within the field of [neuroscience](/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience").

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/The_Galaxy_Within.jpg/330px-The_Galaxy_Within.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Galaxy_Within.jpg)

Astrocytes (green) in the context of neurons (red) in a mouse cortex [cell culture](/wiki/Cell_culture "Cell culture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Human_astrocyte.png/330px-Human_astrocyte.png)](/wiki/File:Human_astrocyte.png)

23-week-old fetal brain culture human astrocyte

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/4c/Astrocytes-mouse-cortex.png)](/wiki/File:Astrocytes-mouse-cortex.png)

Astrocytes (red-yellow) among neurons (green) in the living cerebral cortex

Astrocytes are a sub-type of [glial cells](/wiki/Glial_cells "Glial cells") in the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system"). They are also known as *astrocytic glial cells.* Star-shaped, their many processes envelop [synapses](/wiki/Synapses "Synapses") made by neurons. In humans, a single astrocyte cell can interact with up to 2 million synapses at a time.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Astrocytes are classically identified using [histological](/wiki/Histological "Histological") analysis; many of these cells express the intermediate filament [glial fibrillary acidic protein](/wiki/Glial_fibrillary_acidic_protein "Glial fibrillary acidic protein") (GFAP).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Types")]

Several forms of astrocytes exist in the central nervous system: including *fibrous* (in [white matter](/wiki/White_matter "White matter")), *protoplasmic* (in [grey matter](/wiki/Grey_matter "Grey matter")), and *radial*.

#### Fibrous glia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Fibrous glia")]

The fibrous glia are usually located within white matter, have relatively few [organelles](/wiki/Organelles "Organelles"), and exhibit long unbranched cellular processes. This type often has **astrocytic endfeet processes** that physically connect the cells to the outside of [capillary](/wiki/Capillary "Capillary") walls when they are in proximity to them.[[10]](#cite_note-Koch-10)

#### Protoplasmic glia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Protoplasmic glia")]

The protoplasmic glia are the most prevalent and are found in grey matter tissue, possess a larger quantity of organelles, and exhibit short and highly branched tertiary processes.[[11]](#cite_note-ISBN_9780123858702-11)

#### Radial glia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Radial glia")]

The [radial glial cells](/wiki/Radial_glial_cell "Radial glial cell") are disposed in planes perpendicular to the axes of [ventricles](/wiki/Ventricular_system "Ventricular system"). One of their processes abuts the [pia mater](/wiki/Pia_mater "Pia mater"), while the other is deeply buried in grey matter. Radial glia are mostly present during development, playing a role in [neuron migration](/wiki/Neuron_migration "Neuron migration"). [Müller cells](/wiki/M%C3%BCller_cells "Müller cells") of the [retina](/wiki/Retina "Retina") and [Bergmann glia cells](/wiki/Radial_glial_cell#Bergmann_glia "Radial glial cell") of the [cerebellar cortex](/wiki/Cerebellum "Cerebellum") represent an exception, being present still during adulthood. When in proximity to the pia mater, all three forms of astrocytes send out processes to form the [pia-glial membrane](/wiki/Glia_limitans "Glia limitans").[[11]](#cite_note-ISBN_9780123858702-11)

### Energy use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Energy use")]

Early assessments of energy use in grey matter signaling suggested that 95% was attributed to neurons and 5% to astrocytes.[[12]](#cite_note-Attwell-12) However, after discovering that action potentials were more efficient than initially believed, the energy budget was adjusted: 70% for dendrites, 15% for axons, and 7% for astrocytes.[[13]](#cite_note-Harris-13) Previous accounts assumed that astrocytes captured synaptic K+ solely via [Kir4.1 channels](/wiki/KCNJ10 "KCNJ10"). However, it's now understood they also utilize [Na+/K+ ATPase](/wiki/Sodium%E2%80%93potassium_pump "Sodium–potassium pump"). Factoring in this active buffering, astrocytic energy demand increases by >200%. This is supported by 3D neuropil reconstructions indicating similar mitochondrial densities in both cell types, as well as cell-specific [transcriptomic](/wiki/Transcriptome "Transcriptome") and [proteomic](/wiki/Proteome "Proteome") data, and [tricarboxylic acid](/wiki/Tricarboxylic_acid "Tricarboxylic acid") cycle rates.[[14]](#cite_note-Barros-14) Therefore "Gram-per-gram, astrocytes turn out to be as expensive as neurons".[[14]](#cite_note-Barros-14)

## Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/%CE%91%CF%83%CF%84%CF%81%CE%BF%CE%BA%CF%8D%CF%84%CF%84%CE%B1%CF%81%CE%B1_%CF%80%CE%BF%CE%BD%CF%84%CE%B9%CE%BA%CE%BF%CF%8D_%CF%83%CE%B5_%CE%BA%CE%B1%CE%BB%CE%BB%CE%B9%CE%AD%CF%81%CE%B3%CE%B5%CE%B9%CE%B1.jpg/250px-%CE%91%CF%83%CF%84%CF%81%CE%BF%CE%BA%CF%8D%CF%84%CF%84%CE%B1%CF%81%CE%B1_%CF%80%CE%BF%CE%BD%CF%84%CE%B9%CE%BA%CE%BF%CF%8D_%CF%83%CE%B5_%CE%BA%CE%B1%CE%BB%CE%BB%CE%B9%CE%AD%CF%81%CE%B3%CE%B5%CE%B9%CE%B1.jpg)](/wiki/File:%CE%91%CF%83%CF%84%CF%81%CE%BF%CE%BA%CF%8D%CF%84%CF%84%CE%B1%CF%81%CE%B1_%CF%80%CE%BF%CE%BD%CF%84%CE%B9%CE%BA%CE%BF%CF%8D_%CF%83%CE%B5_%CE%BA%CE%B1%CE%BB%CE%BB%CE%B9%CE%AD%CF%81%CE%B3%CE%B5%CE%B9%CE%B1.jpg)

Astrocytes are depicted in red. Cell nuclei are depicted in blue. Astrocytes were obtained from brains of newborn mice.

Astrocytes are [macroglial cells](/wiki/Glia#Types "Glia") in the central nervous system. Astrocytes are derived from heterogeneous populations of [progenitor cells](/wiki/Progenitor_cell "Progenitor cell") in the [neuroepithelium](/wiki/Neuroepithelial_cell "Neuroepithelial cell") of the developing central nervous system. There is remarkable similarity between the well known genetic mechanisms that specify the lineage of diverse [neuron](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron") subtypes and that of macroglial cells.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Just as with neuronal cell specification, canonical signaling factors like [sonic hedgehog](/wiki/Sonic_hedgehog "Sonic hedgehog") (SHH), [fibroblast growth factor](/wiki/Fibroblast_growth_factor "Fibroblast growth factor") (FGFs), [WNTs](/wiki/Wnt_signaling_pathway "Wnt signaling pathway") and [bone morphogenetic proteins](/wiki/Bone_morphogenetic_protein "Bone morphogenetic protein") (BMPs), provide positional information to developing macroglial cells through morphogen gradients along the dorsal–ventral, anterior–posterior and medial–lateral axes. The resultant patterning along the neuraxis leads to segmentation of the neuroepithelium into progenitor domains (p0, p1 p2, p3 and pMN) for distinct neuron types in the developing spinal cord. On the basis of several studies it is now believed that this model also applies to macroglial cell specification. Studies carried out by Hochstim and colleagues have demonstrated that three distinct populations of astrocytes arise from the p1, p2 and p3 domains.[[16]](#cite_note-16) These subtypes of astrocytes can be identified on the basis of their expression of different transcription factors (PAX6, [NKX6.1](/wiki/NKX6-1 "NKX6-1")) and cell surface markers ([reelin](/wiki/Reelin "Reelin") and [SLIT1](/wiki/SLIT1 "SLIT1")). The three populations of astrocyte subtypes which have been identified are: 1) dorsally located VA1 astrocytes, derived from p1 domain, express [PAX6](/wiki/PAX6 "PAX6") and reelin; 2) ventrally located VA3 astrocytes, derived from p3, express NKX6.1 and SLIT1; and 3) intermediate white-matter located VA2 astrocyte, derived from the p2 domain, which express PAX6, NKX6.1, reelin and SLIT1.[[17]](#cite_note-17) After astrocyte specification has occurred in the developing CNS, it is believed that astrocyte precursors migrate to their final positions within the nervous system before the process of terminal [differentiation](/wiki/Cellular_differentiation "Cellular differentiation") occurs.

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Function")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Metabolic_interactions_between_astrocytes_and_neurons_with_major_reactions.png/960px-Metabolic_interactions_between_astrocytes_and_neurons_with_major_reactions.png)](/wiki/File:Metabolic_interactions_between_astrocytes_and_neurons_with_major_reactions.png)

Metabolic interactions between astrocytes and neurons[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Astrocytes help form the physical structure of the brain, and are thought to play a number of active roles, including the secretion or absorption of neural transmitters and maintenance of the blood–brain barrier.[[19]](#cite_note-19) The concept of a [tripartite synapse](/wiki/Tripartite_synapse "Tripartite synapse") has been proposed, referring to the tight relationship occurring at synapses among a presynaptic element, a postsynaptic element, and a glial element.[[20]](#cite_note-Araque-20)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Astrocyte_endfeet_processes.webp/250px-Astrocyte_endfeet_processes.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Astrocyte_endfeet_processes.webp)

Astrocyte endfeet processes surrounding a blood vessel

* **Structural**: They are involved in the physical structuring of the brain. Astrocytes get their name because they are star-shaped. They are the most abundant glial cells in the brain that are closely associated with neuronal synapses. They regulate the transmission of electrical impulses within the brain.
* **Glycogen fuel reserve buffer**: Astrocytes contain [glycogen](/wiki/Glycogen "Glycogen") and are capable of [gluconeogenesis](/wiki/Gluconeogenesis "Gluconeogenesis"). The astrocytes next to neurons in the [frontal cortex](/wiki/Frontal_cortex "Frontal cortex") and [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus") store and release glucose. Thus, astrocytes can fuel neurons with glucose during periods of high rate of glucose consumption and glucose shortage. A recent research on rats suggests there may be a connection between this activity and physical exercise.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* **Metabolic support**: They provide neurons with nutrients such as [lactate](/wiki/Lactic_acid "Lactic acid").
* **Glucose sensing**: normally associated with neurons, the detection of interstitial glucose levels within the brain is also controlled by astrocytes. Astrocytes *in vitro* become activated by low glucose and are *in vivo* this activation increases gastric emptying to increase digestion.[[22]](#cite_note-22)
* **Blood–brain barrier**: The astrocyte endfeet processes encircling [endothelial cells](/wiki/Endothelial_cell "Endothelial cell") were thought to aid in the maintenance of the [blood–brain barrier](/wiki/Blood%E2%80%93brain_barrier "Blood–brain barrier"), and recent research indicates that they do play a substantial role, along with the [tight junctions](/wiki/Tight_junctions "Tight junctions") and [basal lamina](/wiki/Basal_lamina "Basal lamina").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, it has recently been shown that astrocyte activity is linked to blood flow in the brain, and that this is what is actually being measured in fMRI.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-pmid21314846-24)
* **Transmitter uptake and release**: Astrocytes express plasma membrane transporters for several neurotransmitters, including [glutamate](/wiki/Glutamate_transporter "Glutamate transporter"), ATP, and [GABA](/wiki/GABA_transporter_type_3 "GABA transporter type 3"). More recently, astrocytes were shown to release glutamate or [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") in a vesicular, Ca2+-dependent manner.[[25]](#cite_note-25) (This has been disputed for hippocampal astrocytes.)[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* **Regulation of ion concentration in the extracellular space**: Astrocytes express [potassium channels](/wiki/Potassium_channels "Potassium channels") at a high density. When neurons are active, they [release potassium](/wiki/Potassium_spatial_buffering "Potassium spatial buffering"), increasing the local extracellular concentration. Because astrocytes are highly permeable to potassium, they rapidly clear the excess accumulation in the extracellular space.[[27]](#cite_note-27) If this function is interfered with, the extracellular concentration of potassium will rise, leading to neuronal depolarization by the [Goldman equation](/wiki/Goldman_equation "Goldman equation"). Abnormal accumulation of extracellular potassium is well known to result in epileptic neuronal activity.[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* **Modulation of synaptic transmission**: In the [supraoptic nucleus](/wiki/Supraoptic_nucleus "Supraoptic nucleus") of the [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus"), rapid changes in astrocyte morphology have been shown to affect heterosynaptic transmission between neurons.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In the [hippocampus](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus"), astrocytes suppress synaptic transmission by releasing ATP, which is [hydrolyzed](/wiki/ATP_hydrolysis "ATP hydrolysis") by [ectonucleotidases](/wiki/Ectonucleotidase "Ectonucleotidase") to yield [adenosine](/wiki/Adenosine "Adenosine"). Adenosine acts on neuronal [adenosine receptors](/wiki/Adenosine_receptor "Adenosine receptor") to inhibit synaptic transmission, thereby increasing the [dynamic range](/wiki/Dynamic_range "Dynamic range") available for [LTP](/wiki/Long-term_potentiation "Long-term potentiation").[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* **Vasomodulation**: Astrocytes may serve as intermediaries in neuronal regulation of blood flow.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
* **Promotion of the myelinating activity of oligodendrocytes**: Electrical activity in neurons causes them to release ATP, which serves as an important stimulus for myelin to form. However, the ATP does not act directly on [oligodendrocytes](/wiki/Oligodendrocyte "Oligodendrocyte"). Instead, it causes astrocytes to secrete cytokine leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF), a regulatory protein that promotes the myelinating activity of oligodendrocytes. This suggests that astrocytes have an executive-coordinating role in the brain.[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* **Nervous system repair**: Upon injury to nerve cells within the central nervous system, astrocytes fill up the space to form a [glial scar](/wiki/Glial_scar "Glial scar"), and may contribute to neural repair. The role of astrocytes in CNS regeneration following injury is not well understood though. The glial scar has traditionally been described as an impermeable barrier to regeneration, thus implicating a negative role in axon regeneration. However, recently, it was found through genetic ablation studies that astrocytes are actually required for regeneration to occur.[[33]](#cite_note-pmid27027288-33) More importantly, the authors found that the astrocyte scar is actually essential for stimulated axons (that axons that have been coaxed to grow via neurotrophic supplementation) to extend through the injured spinal cord.[[33]](#cite_note-pmid27027288-33) Astrocytes that have been pushed into a reactive phenotype (termed [astrogliosis](/wiki/Astrogliosis "Astrogliosis"), defined by the upregulation of among others, GFAP and [vimentin](/wiki/Vimentin "Vimentin")[[34]](#cite_note-34) expression, a definition still under debate) may actually be toxic to neurons, releasing signals that can kill neurons.[[35]](#cite_note-pmid28099414-35) Much work, however, remains to elucidate their role in nervous system injury.
* **Long-term potentiation**: There is debate among scientists as to whether astrocytes integrate learning and memory in the hippocampus. Recently, it has been shown that engrafting human glial progenitor cell in nascent mice brains causes the cells to differentiate into astrocytes. After differentiation, these cells increase LTP and improve memory performance in the mice.[[36]](#cite_note-36)
* **Circadian clock**: Astrocytes alone are sufficient to drive the molecular oscillations in the SCN and circadian behavior in mice, and thus can autonomously initiate and sustain complex mammalian behavior.[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* **The switch of the nervous system**: Based on the evidence listed below, it has been recently conjectured in,[[38]](#cite_note-NossensonMagalMesser2016-38) that macro glia (and astrocytes in particular) act both as a lossy neurotransmitter capacitor and as the logical switch of the nervous system. I.e., macroglia either block or enable the propagation of the stimulus along the nervous system, depending on their membrane state and the level of the stimulus.

Astrocytes are linked by [gap junctions](/wiki/Gap_junction "Gap junction"), creating an electrically coupled (functional) [syncytium](/wiki/Syncytium "Syncytium").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Because of this ability of astrocytes to communicate with their neighbors, changes in the activity of one astrocyte can have repercussions on the activities of others that are quite distant from the original astrocyte.

An influx of Ca2+ ions into astrocytes is the essential change that ultimately generates calcium waves. Because this influx is directly caused by an increase in blood flow to the brain, calcium waves are said to be a kind of [hemodynamic response function](/wiki/Hemodynamic_response "Hemodynamic response"). An increase in intracellular calcium concentration can propagate outwards through this functional syncytium. Mechanisms of calcium wave propagation include diffusion of calcium ions and [IP3](/wiki/Inositol_trisphosphate "Inositol trisphosphate") through gap junctions and [extracellular ATP signalling](/wiki/P2RX7#Recruitment_of_pannexin "P2RX7").[[49]](#cite_note-49) Calcium elevations are the primary known axis of activation in astrocytes, and are necessary and sufficient for some types of astrocytic glutamate release.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Given the importance of calcium signaling in astrocytes, tight regulatory mechanisms for the progression of the spatio-temporal calcium signaling have been developed. Via mathematical analysis it has been shown that localized inflow of Ca2+ ions yields a localized raise in the cytosolic concentration of Ca2+ ions.[[51]](#cite_note-Spatial-51) Moreover, cytosolic Ca2+ accumulation is independent of every intracellular calcium flux and depends on the Ca2+ exchange across the
membrane, cytosolic calcium diffusion, geometry of the cell, extracellular calcium perturbation, and initial concentrations.[[51]](#cite_note-Spatial-51)

### Tripartite synapse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Tripartite synapse")]

Main article: [Tripartite synapse](/wiki/Tripartite_synapse "Tripartite synapse")

Within the dorsal horn of the [spinal cord](/wiki/Spinal_cord "Spinal cord"), activated astrocytes have the ability to respond to almost all [neurotransmitters](/wiki/Neurotransmitters "Neurotransmitters")[[52]](#cite_note-Haydon2001-52) and, upon activation, release a multitude of neuroactive molecules such as [glutamate](/wiki/Glutamate "Glutamate"), [ATP](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate"), [nitric oxide](/wiki/Nitric_oxide "Nitric oxide") (NO), and [prostaglandins](/wiki/Prostaglandins "Prostaglandins") (PG), which in turn influences neuronal excitability. The close association between astrocytes and [presynaptic](/wiki/Presynaptic "Presynaptic") and [postsynaptic](/wiki/Postsynaptic "Postsynaptic") terminals as well as their ability to integrate synaptic activity and release neuromodulators has been termed the [tripartite synapse](/wiki/Tripartite_synapse "Tripartite synapse").[[20]](#cite_note-Araque-20) Synaptic modulation by astrocytes takes place because of this three-part association.

A 2023 study suggested astrocytes, previously underexplored brain cells, could be key to extending wakefulness without negative effects on cognition and health.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Glutamatergic gliotransmission

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Glutamatergic gliotransmission")]

Some specialized astrocytes mediate [glutamatergic](/wiki/Glutamatergic "Glutamatergic") [gliotransmission](/wiki/Gliotransmission "Gliotransmission") in the [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system").[[54]](#cite_note-54) Such cells have been called **hybrid brain cells** because they exhibit both neuron-like and [glial-like properties](/wiki/Glia "Glia"). Unlike traditional [neurons](/wiki/Neuron "Neuron"), these [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") not only transmit electrical signals but also provide supportive roles typically associated with [glial cells](/wiki/Glia "Glia"), such as regulating the brain's extracellular environment and maintaining overall homeostasis.[[55]](#cite_note-:0-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

## Clinical significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Clinical significance")]

### Astrocytomas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Astrocytomas")]

[Astrocytomas](/wiki/Astrocytoma "Astrocytoma") are [primary tumors](/wiki/Primary_tumor "Primary tumor") [in the CNS](/wiki/Nervous_system_tumor#In_the_CNS "Nervous system tumor") that develop from astrocytes. It is also possible that [glial progenitors](/wiki/Radial_glial_cell "Radial glial cell") or [neural stem cells](/wiki/Neural_stem_cell "Neural stem cell") can give rise to astrocytomas. These tumors may occur in many parts of the brain or spinal cord. Astrocytomas are divided into two categories: low grade (I and II) and high grade (III and IV). Low grade tumors are more common in children, and high grade tumors are more common in adults. Malignant astrocytomas are more prevalent among men, contributing to worse survival.[[58]](#cite_note-ref32-58)

[Pilocytic astrocytomas](/wiki/Pilocytic_astrocytoma "Pilocytic astrocytoma") are grade I tumors. They are considered benign and slow growing tumors. Pilocytic astrocytomas frequently have cystic portions filled with fluid and a nodule, which is the solid portion. Most are located in the cerebellum. Therefore, most symptoms are related to balance or coordination difficulties.[[58]](#cite_note-ref32-58) They also occur more frequently in children and teens.[[59]](#cite_note-ref33-59)

[Fibrillary astrocytomas](/wiki/Fibrillary_astrocytoma "Fibrillary astrocytoma") are grade II tumors. They grow relatively slowly so are usually considered benign, but they infiltrate the surrounding healthy tissue and can become [malignant](/wiki/Malignancy "Malignancy"). Fibrillary astrocytomas commonly occur in younger people, who often present with seizures.[[59]](#cite_note-ref33-59)

[Anaplastic astrocytomas](/wiki/Anaplastic_astrocytoma "Anaplastic astrocytoma") are grade III malignant tumors. They grow more rapidly than lower grade tumors. Anaplastic astrocytomas recur more frequently than lower grade tumors because their tendency to spread into surrounding tissue makes them difficult to completely remove surgically.[[58]](#cite_note-ref32-58)

[Glioblastoma](/wiki/Glioblastoma "Glioblastoma") is a grade IV cancer that may originate from astrocytes or an existing astrocytoma. Approximately 50% of all brain tumors are glioblastomas. Glioblastomas can contain multiple glial cell types, including astrocytes and [oligodendrocytes](/wiki/Oligodendrocyte "Oligodendrocyte"). Glioblastomas are generally considered to be the most invasive type of glial tumor, as they grow rapidly and spread to nearby tissue. Treatment may be complicated, because one tumor cell type may die off in response to a particular treatment while the other cell types may continue to multiply.[[58]](#cite_note-ref32-58)

### Neurodevelopmental disorders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Neurodevelopmental disorders")]

Astrocytes have emerged as important participants in various [neurodevelopmental disorders](/wiki/Neurodevelopmental_disorder "Neurodevelopmental disorder"). This view states that astrocyte dysfunction may result in improper neural circuitry, which underlies certain psychiatric disorders such as [autism spectrum disorders](/wiki/Autism_spectrum_disorders "Autism spectrum disorders") and [schizophrenia](/wiki/Schizophrenia "Schizophrenia").[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[5]](#cite_note-Sloan-5)

### Chronic pain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Chronic pain")]

Under normal conditions, pain conduction begins with some noxious signal followed by an action potential carried by [nociceptive](/wiki/Nociceptive "Nociceptive") (pain sensing) afferent neurons, which elicit [excitatory postsynaptic potentials](/wiki/Excitatory_postsynaptic_potentials "Excitatory postsynaptic potentials") (EPSP) in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord. That message is then relayed to the [cerebral cortex](/wiki/Cerebral_cortex "Cerebral cortex"), where we translate those EPSPs into "pain". Since the discovery of astrocyte-neuron signaling, our understanding of the conduction of pain has been dramatically complicated. Pain processing is no longer seen as a repetitive relay of signals from body to brain, but as a complex system that can be up- and down-regulated by a number of different factors. One factor at the forefront of recent research is in the pain-potentiating synapse located in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord and the role of astrocytes in encapsulating these synapses. Garrison and co-workers[[61]](#cite_note-61) were the first to suggest association when they found a correlation between astrocyte [hypertrophy](/wiki/Hypertrophy "Hypertrophy") in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord and hypersensitivity to pain after peripheral nerve injury, typically considered an indicator of glial activation after injury. Astrocytes detect neuronal activity and can release chemical transmitters, which in turn control synaptic activity.[[52]](#cite_note-Haydon2001-52)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) In the past, [hyperalgesia](/wiki/Hyperalgesia "Hyperalgesia") was thought to be modulated by the release of [substance P](/wiki/Substance_P "Substance P") and excitatory amino acids (EAA), such as [glutamate](/wiki/Glutamate "Glutamate"), from the presynaptic afferent nerve terminals in the spinal cord dorsal horn. Subsequent activation of [AMPA](/wiki/AMPA "AMPA") (α-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-4-isoxazole propionic acid), [NMDA](/wiki/NMDA "NMDA") (N-methyl-D-aspartate) and [kainate](/wiki/Kainate "Kainate") subtypes of ionotropic [glutamate receptors](/wiki/Glutamate_receptor "Glutamate receptor") follows. It is the activation of these receptors that potentiates the pain signal up the spinal cord. This idea, although true, is an oversimplification of pain transduction. A litany of other neurotransmitter and neuromodulators, such as [calcitonin gene-related peptide](/wiki/Calcitonin_gene-related_peptide "Calcitonin gene-related peptide") (CGRP), [adenosine triphosphate](/wiki/Adenosine_triphosphate "Adenosine triphosphate") (ATP), [brain-derived neurotrophic factor](/wiki/Brain-derived_neurotrophic_factor "Brain-derived neurotrophic factor") (BDNF), [somatostatin](/wiki/Somatostatin "Somatostatin"), [vasoactive intestinal peptide](/wiki/Vasoactive_intestinal_peptide "Vasoactive intestinal peptide") (VIP), [galanin](/wiki/Galanin "Galanin"), and [vasopressin](/wiki/Vasopressin "Vasopressin") are all synthesized and released in response to [noxious stimuli](/wiki/Noxious_stimulus "Noxious stimulus"). In addition to each of these regulatory factors, several other interactions between pain-transmitting neurons and other neurons in the dorsal horn have added impact on pain pathways.

#### Two states of persistent pain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Two states of persistent pain")]

After persistent peripheral tissue damage there is a release of several factors from the injured tissue as well as in the spinal dorsal horn. These factors increase the responsiveness of the dorsal horn pain-projection neurons to ensuing stimuli, termed "spinal sensitization", thus amplifying the pain impulse to the brain. Release of glutamate, substance P, and calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) mediates NMDAR activation (originally silent because it is plugged by Mg2+), thus aiding in depolarization of the postsynaptic pain-transmitting neurons (PTN). In addition, activation of IP3 signaling and [MAPKs](/wiki/MAPK "MAPK") (mitogen-activated protein kinases) such as [ERK](/wiki/Extracellular_signal-regulated_kinases "Extracellular signal-regulated kinases") and [JNK](/wiki/JNK "JNK"), bring about an increase in the synthesis of inflammatory factors that alter glutamate transporter function. ERK also further activates AMPARs and NMDARs in neurons. [Nociception](/wiki/Nociception "Nociception") is further sensitized by the association of ATP and substance P with their respective receptors (P2X3) and [neurokinin 1 receptor](/wiki/Neurokinin_1_receptor "Neurokinin 1 receptor") (NK1R), as well as activation of [metabotropic glutamate receptors](/wiki/Metabotropic_glutamate_receptors "Metabotropic glutamate receptors") and release of BDNF. Persistent presence of glutamate in the synapse eventually results in dysregulation of [GLT1](/wiki/GLT1 "GLT1") and [GLAST](/wiki/Glutamate_aspartate_transporter "Glutamate aspartate transporter"), crucial transporters of glutamate into astrocytes. Ongoing excitation can also induce ERK and JNK activation, resulting in release of several inflammatory factors.

As noxious pain is sustained, spinal sensitization creates [transcriptional changes](/wiki/Transcriptional_regulation "Transcriptional regulation") in the neurons of the dorsal horn that lead to altered function for extended periods. Mobilization of Ca2+ from internal stores results from persistent synaptic activity and leads to the release of glutamate, ATP, tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), interleukin 1β ([IL-1β](/wiki/Interleukin_1_family "Interleukin 1 family")), IL-6, nitric oxide (NO), and prostaglandin E2 (PGE2). Activated astrocytes are also a source of [matrix metalloproteinase](/wiki/Matrix_metalloproteinase "Matrix metalloproteinase") 2 ([MMP2](/wiki/MMP2 "MMP2")), which induces [pro-IL-1β](/wiki/Pro-IL-1%CE%B2 "Pro-IL-1β") cleavage and sustains astrocyte activation. In this chronic signaling pathway, p38 is activated as a result of [IL-1β](/wiki/Interleukin_1_family "Interleukin 1 family") signaling, and there is a presence of chemokines that trigger their receptors to become active. In response to nerve damage, [heat shock proteins](/wiki/Heat_shock_proteins "Heat shock proteins") (HSP) are released and can bind to their respective [TLRs](/wiki/Toll-like_receptor "Toll-like receptor"), leading to further activation.

### Other pathologies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Other pathologies")]

See also: [Neuroimmune system](/wiki/Neuroimmune_system "Neuroimmune system")

Other clinically significant pathologies involving astrocytes include [astrogliosis](/wiki/Astrogliosis "Astrogliosis") and [astrocytopathy](/wiki/Astrocytopathy "Astrocytopathy"). Examples of these include [multiple sclerosis](/wiki/Multiple_sclerosis "Multiple sclerosis"), anti-AQP4+ [neuromyelitis optica](/wiki/Neuromyelitis_optica "Neuromyelitis optica"), [Rasmussen's encephalitis](/wiki/Rasmussen%27s_encephalitis "Rasmussen's encephalitis"), [Alexander disease](/wiki/Alexander_disease "Alexander disease"), and [amyotrophic lateral sclerosis](/wiki/Amyotrophic_lateral_sclerosis "Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis").[[64]](#cite_note-64) Studies have shown that astrocytes may be implied in [neurodegenerative diseases](/wiki/Neurodegenerative_disease "Neurodegenerative disease"), such as [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease"),[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66) [Parkinson's disease](/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease "Parkinson's disease"),[[67]](#cite_note-67) [Huntington's disease](/wiki/Huntington%27s_disease "Huntington's disease"), [Stuttering](/wiki/Stuttering "Stuttering")[[68]](#cite_note-68) and [amyotrophic lateral sclerosis](/wiki/Amyotrophic_lateral_sclerosis "Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis"),[[69]](#cite_note-69) and in acute brain injuries, such as intracerebral hemorrhage [[70]](#cite_note-pmid32423330-70) and traumatic brain injury.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

#### Gomori-positive astrocytes and brain dysfunction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Gomori-positive astrocytes and brain dysfunction")]

A type of astrocyte with an aging-related pathology has been described over the last fifty years. Astrocytes of this subtype possess prominent cytoplasmic granules that are intensely stained by Gomori's chrome alum hematoxylin stain, and hence are termed Gomori-positive (GP) astrocytes. They can be found throughout the brain, but are by far the most abundant in the olfactory bulbs, medial habenula, dentate gyrus of the hippocampus, arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus, and in the dorsal medulla, just beneath the area postrema.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

Gomori-positive cytoplasmic granules are derived from damaged mitochondria engulfed within lysosomes.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Cytoplasmic granules contain undigested remnants of mitochondrial structures. These contents include heme-linked copper and iron atoms remaining from mitochondrial enzymes.[[74]](#cite_note-74) These chemical substances account for the pseudoperoxidase activity of Gomori-positive granules that can utilized to stain for these granules. [Oxidative stress](/wiki/Oxidative_stress "Oxidative stress") is believed to be cause of damage to these astrocytes.[[75]](#cite_note-75) However, the exact nature of this stress is uncertain.

Brain regions enriched in Gomori-positive astrocytes also contain a sub-population of specialized astrocytes that synthesize Fatty Acid Binding Protein 7 (FABP7). Indeed, astrocytes in the hypothalamus that synthesize FABP7 have also been shown to possess Gomori-positive granules.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Thus, a connection between these two glial features is apparent. Recent data have shown that astrocytes, but not neurons, possess the mitochondrial enzymes needed to metabolize fatty acids, and that the resulting oxidative stress can damage mitochondria.[[77]](#cite_note-77) Thus, an increased uptake and oxidation of fatty acids in glia containing FABP7 is likely to cause the oxidative stress and damage to mitochondria in these cells. Also, FABP proteins have recently been shown to interact with a protein called synuclein to cause mitochondrial damage.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

#### Possible roles in pathophysiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Possible roles in pathophysiology")]

Astrocytes can transfer mitochondria into adjacent neurons to improve neuronal function.[[79]](#cite_note-79) It is therefore plausible that the damage to astrocyte mitochondria seen in GP astrocytes could affect the activity of neurons.

A number of hypothalamic functions show declines in aging that may be related to GP astrocytes. For example, GP astrocytes are in close contact with neurons that make a neurotransmitter called dopamine in both the rat and human hypothalamus.[[80]](#cite_note-80) The dopamine produced by these neurons is carried to the nearby pituitary gland to inhibit the release of a hormone called prolactin from the pituitary. The activity of dopaminergic neurons declines during aging, leading to elevations in blood levels of prolactin that can provoke breast cancer.[[81]](#cite_note-81) An aging-associated change in astrocyte function might contribute to this change in dopaminergic activity.

FABP7+ astrocytes are in close contact with neurons in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus that are responsive to a hormone called leptin that is produced by fat cells. Leptin-sensitive neurons regulate appetite and body weight. FABP7+ astrocytes regulate the responsiveness of these neurons to leptin. Mitochondrial damage in these astrocytes could thus alter the function of leptin-sensitive neurons and could contribute to an aging-associated dysregulation of feeding and body weight.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

GP astrocytes may also be involved in the hypothalamic regulation of overall glucose metabolism. Recent data show that astrocytes function as glucose sensors and exert a commanding influence upon neuronal reactivity to changes in extracellular glucose.[[83]](#cite_note-83) GP astrocytes possess high-capacity GLUT2-type glucose transporter proteins and appear to modulate the neuronal responses to glucose.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Hypothalamic cells monitor blood levels of glucose and exert an influence upon blood glucose levels via an altered input to autonomic circuits that innervate liver and muscle cells.

The importance of astrocytes in aging-related disturbances in glucose metabolism has been recently illustrated by studies of diabetic animals. A single infusion of a protein called fibroblast growth factor-1 into the hypothalamus has been shown to permanently normalize blood glucose levels in diabetic rodents. This remarkable cure of diabetes mellitus is mediated by astrocytes. The most prominent genes activated by FGF-1 treatment include the genes responsible for the synthesis of FABP6 and FABP7 by astrocytes.[[85]](#cite_note-85) These data confirm the importance of FABP7+ astrocytes for the control of blood glucose. Dysfunction of FABP7+/Gomori-positive astrocytes may contribute to the aging-related development of diabetes mellitus.

GP astrocytes are also present in the dentate gyrus of the hippocampus in both rodent and human brains.[[86]](#cite_note-86) The hippocampus undergoes severe degenerative changes during aging in Alzheimer's disease. The reasons for these degenerative changes are currently being hotly debated. A recent study has shown that levels of glial proteins, and NOT neuronal proteins, are most abnormal in Alzheimer's disease. The glial protein most severely affected is FABP5.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Another study showed that 100% of hippocampal astrocytes that contain FABP7 also contain FABP5.[[88]](#cite_note-88) These data suggest that FABP7+/Gomori-positive astrocytes may play a role in Alzheimer's disease. An altered glial function in this region could compromise the function of dentate gyrus neurons and also the function of axons that terminate in the dentate gyrus. Many such axons originate in the lateral entorhinal cortex, which is the first brain region to show degeneration in Alzheimer's disease. Astrocyte pathology in the hippocampus thus might make a contribution to the pathology of Alzheimer's disease.

## Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Research")]

A study performed in November 2010 and published March 2011, was done by a team of scientists from the [University of Rochester](/wiki/University_of_Rochester "University of Rochester") and [University of Colorado School of Medicine](/wiki/University_of_Colorado_School_of_Medicine "University of Colorado School of Medicine"). They did an experiment to attempt to repair [trauma](/wiki/Trauma_(medicine) "Trauma (medicine)") to the [Central Nervous System](/wiki/Central_Nervous_System "Central Nervous System") of an adult rat by replacing the glial cells. When the glial cells were injected into the injury of the adult rat's spinal cord, astrocytes were generated by exposing human glial precursor cells to bone morphogenetic protein (bone morphogenetic protein is important because it is considered to create [tissue](/wiki/Tissue_(biology) "Tissue (biology)") architecture throughout the body). So, with the bone protein and human glial cells combined, they promoted significant recovery of conscious foot placement, [axonal](/wiki/Axonal "Axonal") growth, and obvious increases in neuronal survival in the spinal cord [laminae](/wiki/Basal_lamina "Basal lamina"). On the other hand, human glial [precursor cells](/wiki/Precursor_cells "Precursor cells") and astrocytes generated from these cells by being in contact with [ciliary](/wiki/Cilium "Cilium") neurotrophic factors, failed to promote neuronal survival and support of axonal growth at the spot of the injury.[[89]](#cite_note-ref34-89)

One study done in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") had two types of [hippocampal](/wiki/Hippocampus "Hippocampus") neuronal cultures: In one culture, the neuron was grown from a layer of astrocytes and the other culture was not in contact with any astrocytes, but they were instead fed a [glial conditioned medium](/w/index.php?title=Glial_conditioned_medium&action=edit&redlink=1 "Glial conditioned medium (page does not exist)") (GCM), which inhibits the rapid growth of cultured astrocytes in the brains of rats in most cases. In their results they were able to see that astrocytes had a direct role in [Long-term potentiation](/wiki/Long-term_potentiation "Long-term potentiation") with the mixed culture (which is the culture that was grown from a layer of astrocytes) but not in GCM cultures.[[90]](#cite_note-ref36-90)

Studies have shown that astrocytes play an important function in the regulation of neural [stem cells](/wiki/Stem_cell "Stem cell"). Research from the Schepens Eye Research Institute at [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") shows the human brain to abound in neural stem cells, which are kept in a dormant state by [chemical signals](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") (ephrin-A2 and ephrin-A3) from the astrocytes. The astrocytes are able to activate the stem cells to transform into working neurons by dampening the release of [ephrin-A2](/wiki/Ephrin-A2 "Ephrin-A2") and [ephrin-A3](/wiki/Ephrin-A3 "Ephrin-A3").[[91]](#cite_note-pmid18562299-91)

In a study published in a 2011 issue of *Nature Biotechnology*[[92]](#cite_note-92) a group of researchers from the University of Wisconsin reports that it has been able to direct embryonic and induced human [stem cells](/wiki/Stem_cells "Stem cells") to become astrocytes.

A 2012 study[[93]](#cite_note-93) of the effects of [marijuana](/wiki/Marijuana "Marijuana") on short-term memories found that [THC](/wiki/THC "THC") activates [CB1](/wiki/Cannabinoid_receptor_1 "Cannabinoid receptor 1") receptors of astrocytes which cause receptors for [AMPA](/wiki/AMPA "AMPA") to be removed from the membranes of associated neurons.

A 2023 study[[94]](#cite_note-94) showed that astrocytes also play an active role in [Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Alzheimer%27s_disease "Alzheimer's disease"). More specifically, when astrocytes became reactive they unleash the pathological effects of [amyloid-beta](/wiki/Amyloid-beta "Amyloid-beta") on downstream tau phosphorylation and deposition, which very likely will lead to cognitive deterioration.

As astrocytes are increasingly recognized as active contributors to several neurological disorders and potential therapeutic targets,[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) researchers are investigating how these cells can be monitored or modulated using emerging neurotechnologies.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-:1-98) For example, a recent *in vitro* study (2025) reported that physiologically relevant concentrations of Ti3C2Tx MXene flakes, a promising two-dimensional nanomaterial for glial-targeted neural interfaces, were biocompatible with astrocytes and did not alter cell viability, morphology, or spontaneous calcium signaling.[[98]](#cite_note-:1-98) Research in this area aims to investigate how astrocytes interact with emerging neurotechnologies and engineered materials, which is increasingly important as tools for understanding and modulating brain function continue to advance.

## Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Classification")]

There are several different ways to classify astrocytes.

### Lineage and antigenic phenotype

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Lineage and antigenic phenotype")]

These have been established by classic work by Raff et al. in early 1980s on Rat optic nerves.

* Type 1: Antigenically Ran2+, GFAP+, FGFR3+, A2B5−, thus resembling the "type 1 astrocyte" of the postnatal day 7 rat optic nerve. These can arise from the tripotential glial restricted precursor cells (GRP), but not from the bipotential O2A/OPC (oligodendrocyte, type 2 astrocyte precursor, also called *[Oligodendrocyte progenitor cell](/wiki/Oligodendrocyte_precursor_cell "Oligodendrocyte precursor cell")*) cells.
* Type 2: Antigenically A2B5+, GFAP+, FGFR3−, Ran 2−. These cells can develop *in vitro* from the either tripotential GRP (probably via O2A stage) or from bipotential O2A cells (which some people{{[[99]](#cite_note-99)}} think may in turn have been derived from the GRP) **or** in vivo when these progenitor cells are transplanted into lesion sites (but *probably not in normal development, at least not in the rat optic nerve*). Type 2 astrocytes are the major astrocytic component in postnatal optic nerve cultures that are generated by O2A cells grown in the presence of fetal calf serum but are not thought to exist *in vivo*.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

### Anatomical classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Anatomical classification")]

* Protoplasmic: found in grey matter and have many branching processes whose end-feet envelop [synapses](/wiki/Synapses "Synapses"). Some protoplasmic astrocytes are generated by multipotent [subventricular zone](/wiki/Subventricular_zone "Subventricular zone") progenitor cells.[[101]](#cite_note-pmid8439409-101)[[102]](#cite_note-pmid7472478-102)
* [Gömöri](/wiki/Gy%C3%B6rgy_G%C3%B6m%C3%B6ri "György Gömöri")-positive astrocytes. These are a subset of protoplasmic astrocytes that contain numerous cytoplasmic inclusions, or granules, that stain positively with [Gömöri trichrome stain](/wiki/G%C3%B6m%C3%B6ri_trichrome_stain "Gömöri trichrome stain") a chrome-alum hematoxylin [stain](/wiki/Staining "Staining"). It is now known that these granules are formed from the remnants of degenerating mitochondria engulfed within lysosomes,[[103]](#cite_note-103) Some type of oxidative stress appears to be responsible for the mitochondrial damage within these specialized astrocytes. Gömöri-positive astrocytes are much more abundant within the [arcuate nucleus](/wiki/Arcuate_nucleus "Arcuate nucleus") of the [hypothalamus](/wiki/Hypothalamus "Hypothalamus") and in the hippocampus than in other brain regions. They may have a role in regulating the response of the hypothalamus to glucose.[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105)
* Fibrous: found in white matter and have long thin unbranched processes whose end-feet envelop [nodes of Ranvier](/wiki/Nodes_of_Ranvier "Nodes of Ranvier"). Some fibrous astrocytes are generated by [radial glia](/wiki/Radial_glia "Radial glia").[[106]](#cite_note-pmid77708-106)[[107]](#cite_note-pmid111580-107)[[108]](#cite_note-pmid3069243-108)[[109]](#cite_note-pmid2808761-109)[[110]](#cite_note-pmid8844514-110)

### Transporter/receptor classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Astrocyte&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Transporter/receptor classification")]

* GluT type: these express [glutamate transporters](/wiki/Glutamate_transporter "Glutamate transporter") ([EAAT1](/wiki/EAAT1 "EAAT1")/*[SLC1A3](https://www.genenames.org/tools/search/#!/genes?query=SLC1A3)* and [EAAT2](/wiki/EAAT2 "EAAT2")/*[SLC1A2](https://www.genenames.org/tools/search/#!/genes?query=SLC1A2)*) and respond to synaptic release of glutamate by transporter currents. The function and availability of EAAT2 is modulated by [TAAR1](/wiki/TAAR1 "TAAR1"), an intracellular receptor in human astrocytes.[[111]](#cite_note-Astrocyte-localized_TAAR1-111)
* GluR type: these express [glutamate receptors](/wiki/Glutamate_receptors "Glutamate receptors") (mostly [mGluR](/wiki/Metabotropic_glutamate_receptor "Metabotropic glutamate receptor") and [AMPA](/wiki/AMPA_receptor "AMPA receptor") type) and respond to synaptic release of glutamate by channel-mediated currents and [IP3](/wiki/Inositol_triphosphate "Inositol triphosphate")-dependent Ca2+ transients.

"Moreover, TAAR1 overexpression significantly decreased EAAT-2 levels and glutamate clearance that were further reduced by METH. Taken together, our data show that METH treatment activated TAAR1 leading to intracellular cAMP in human astrocytes and modulated glutamate clearance abilities. Furthermore, molecular alterations in astrocyte TAAR1 levels correspond to changes in astrocyte EAAT-2 levels and function."
